trajectory_summary: This is a 40-second routing call that never became a qualifying call. The rep discovered mid-sentence that the customer was an existing ION customer, improvised a cover story ('annual checkup'), and transferred without executing a single orphaned_flow step. The call shape is stagnant — no energy was built or leaked because no real conversation occurred. The primary failure is pre-call preparation: the rep should have known this customer's status before dialing.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the outcome is a blind transfer — the rep routed an existing customer to a specialist without confirming system status, ownership type, or original installer. This is not a booked appointment and not a clean DQ. It is an unqualified handoff with unknown downstream risk.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Orphaned-flow protocol execution on existing-customer trigger
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. So it looks like we were just trying to reach you because we had put together, well, sorry. Do you have solar panels with us already, the ION Solar?
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep discovered the customer's existing-customer status live, improvised a cover story, and transferred without running a single orphaned_flow step — including the system-working question that carries a hard DQ. Before coaching the rep on the protocol itself, the manager should verify whether the CRM record was flagged before the call was assigned. If it was flagged, the coaching conversation is about pre-call prep discipline. If it was not flagged, the conversation is about equipping the rep to execute orphaned_flow even when surprised mid-call — using the 'annual checkup' framing she already improvised as the natural entry point for the four qualification questions.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. So it looks like we were just trying to reach you because we had put together, well, sorry. Do you have solar panels with us already, the ION Solar?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep's mid-sentence self-correction is the tell: she did not know this was an existing customer before dialing. The orphaned_flow protocol exists precisely for this trigger — when a customer confirms existing solar, the rep is supposed to run four qualification steps (own/lease, original company, panel size, system working) before any routing decision. None of those steps were attempted. Strategic read: the rep may have been working from a recycled or misrouted lead with no CRM flag — in which case this is a systems failure, not a rep failure. Reactive read: the rep skipped pre-call record review and discovered the customer's status live. Both branches are possible; the coaching focus should address both conditionally.

This single-word confirmation is the orphaned_flow trigger. The schema requires four sequential questions once existing solar ownership is confirmed: (1) own or lease, (2) original company, (3) panel size, (4) system working. The fourth question carries a hard DQ — if the system is broken, Ion cannot service it and the transfer is contraindicated. The rep skipped all four and transferred anyway. The most consequential skip is question four: the specialist receiving this transfer has no idea whether the system is functional.

key_moments.quote: Okay. So, yeah, one of my specialists was just trying to reach out to you and kinda do, like, an annual checkup and make sure that the system is working well and everything.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep invented a plausible-sounding reason for the call ('annual checkup') rather than following the orphaned_flow diagnostic. The hedge language ('kinda do, like') is the audio signal of improvisation under uncertainty. This is not a rebuttal or a strategic deferral — it is a rep filling dead air with a cover story because she does not have a prepared protocol for this scenario. The cover story is not harmful on its own, but it bypasses the four qualification steps that should follow the existing-customer trigger.

This is a boundary-setting statement consistent with the schema's time_constraint protocol. The customer is not signaling enthusiasm or expanded availability — she is capping the interaction. The rep's response ('Okay. Perfect. I will transfer you right now.') treats the time constraint as confirmation to proceed with transfer rather than as a prompt to triage quickly. The schema's time_constraint protocol calls for a firm time anchor ('It's a 30-minute call — we'll get you in and out') — but that protocol applies to appointment-setting, not existing-customer routing. In this context, the five-minute window was actually sufficient to run the four orphaned_flow questions (estimated 90 seconds) before transferring. The rep used the time constraint as an exit accelerant rather than a triage prompt.
